name: Priya Nair
chief_complaint: I have this sharp pain in my side when I breathe in, and I've been feeling really short of breath since yesterday
onset_description: Noticed mild shortness of breath yesterday evening that she attributed to jet lag. Woke up this morning with sudden sharp right-sided chest pain worse with deep breathing. Became increasingly short of breath over a few hours. Returned from a 14-hour flight from Mumbai 3 days ago.

pleuritic_chest_pain.description: Sharp, stabbing pain on the right side of my chest, it catches every time I take a breath in

dyspnea.description: I feel like I can't get enough air, even just sitting still. It's not normal for me.
dyspnea.onset: Yesterday evening, worsened significantly this morning
dyspnea.character: air hunger at rest, worse with exertion

tachycardia_sensation.description: My heart has been racing all morning, I can feel it pounding

right_calf_pain.description: Now that you mention it, my right calf has been sore and a little swollen since the flight. I thought it was just from sitting so long.
right_calf_pain.onset: Noticed 2 days ago after the flight
associated_symptoms: mild warmth to touch in calf area

anxiety.description: I'm very worried. I'm normally healthy, this isn't like me at all.

labs.troponin: 0.08 ng/mL (mildly elevated, suggests right heart strain)
labs.BNP: 210 pg/mL (mildly elevated, suggests right ventricular strain)
labs.CBC: WBC 10.2, Hgb 13.8, Plt 265 - within normal limits
labs.ABG: pH 7.47, pCO2 30 mmHg, pO2 68 mmHg (respiratory alkalosis with hypoxemia)
labs.CT_angiography_chest: Bilateral pulmonary emboli: large saddle embolus at main pulmonary artery bifurcation with extension into right and left main pulmonary arteries. Right ventricle enlarged (RV/LV ratio 1.3).
labs.lower_extremity_duplex: Acute deep vein thrombosis in right popliteal and posterior tibial veins

medications: Combined oral contraceptive pill (ethinyl estradiol 30mcg/norgestimate 0.25mg) â€” for 6 years
Sumatriptan 50mg PRN for migraines (uses 1-2x per month)

social_history.occupation: Software engineer at a tech company, mostly desk work. Frequent international travel for work.
social_history.living_situation: Lives with her husband in a two-bedroom condo
social_history.exercise: Usually does yoga 3x/week and walks daily, has been sedentary since the trip due to fatigue

conditions.maternal_aunt: Had a DVT at age 42 (no further workup done)

personality: calm, analytical, and detail-oriented â€” approaches her own symptoms almost like debugging a problem. Polite and respectful but asks a lot of thoughtful questions about what tests mean and what the plan is.
communication_style: Articulate and precise. Provides well-organized answers with timelines. Will ask clarifying questions back. May downplay the calf pain unless specifically asked about leg symptoms. Uses some medical terminology she's picked up from reading online but is not a medical professional.
pain_description_style: Very specific and descriptive. 'It's a sharp 7 out of 10 that's localized to my right lower chest and worsens on inspiration.' Comfortable with rating scales. Will mention she looked up her symptoms online and is worried about a blood clot.

critical_findings: Bilateral pulmonary emboli with saddle embolus â€” submassive PE
Right ventricular enlargement (RV/LV ratio 1.3) indicating right heart strain
Elevated troponin and BNP confirming hemodynamic significance
Concurrent DVT in right popliteal and posterior tibial veins
Major risk factors: combined OCP use + prolonged immobilization (14-hour flight)
SpO2 92% with tachycardia at 118 â€” hemodynamically significant
rubric.diagnosis: Submassive (Intermediate-Risk) Pulmonary Embolism with concurrent DVT
recommended_interventions: Immediate systemic anticoagulation with IV heparin drip (weight-based protocol)
IV access with fluid resuscitation (cautious â€” avoid volume overload with RV strain)
Monitor for hemodynamic deterioration â€” if develops hypotension, escalate to massive PE protocol (consider thrombolytics)
Pain management (avoid NSAIDs if anticoagulated â€” use acetaminophen)
Hypercoagulability workup after acute phase (Factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene mutation, antiphospholipid antibodies)
Transition to oral anticoagulation (rivaroxaban or warfarin with heparin bridge) when stable
